just grabbing some coffee.

problem.

Just a quick intro for myself.
My name is Matthew.

I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX, which means that I'm in, theoretically, in charge of the, like, quality of the content, content deliverables on the client side, kind of articulating what best practices are there, establishing, you know, our idea of good, et cetera, there.
So that deals with process and dealing with content managers and dealing with the outputs that they're giving to clients.

But then also working backwards from there, understanding what makes good content, articulating that, and then making sure that that makes its way into the engineering team's ideas of, what they're...

We're doing with the pipelines and how our automation tooling and pipelines are generating that content, hopefully closing the gap between what we would love to deliver to a client and the very thing that is coming out of the pipeline and minimizing the amount of human intervention that has to be done to handle tasks that are repeatable.

And instead leave them free to take action on human intelligence, non-repeatable tasks like stylistic decisions or, you know, high level strategic changes to articles that make them more suited for the client's goals.

So it's a lot, a mouthful, but basically it's like content, articulating how good that content is and then making sure that the pipelines are improved in a way that positively affects the output of the pipelines in a way that hopefully improves client delivery.
So that's my role here.
Previous to this, I was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for about 10 years, ran editorial operations and global events and stuff there.
And then did some startup marketing between.
Before that, was a founder, founded a couple of companies.
So that's a snapshot of me.
I just want to give you a little bit of context who you're talking to.
And now I'm happy to hear a little bit about you and where you've been, what you're up to.
Okay.
So I've been working in content marketing for 10 years.
A balance between being a freelancer and working at marketing and advertising agencies.
So I've never worked client-side.
I've either worked for myself or worked at agencies for other clients.
Most specifically over the last five years, I've been mostly working in the tech space, primarily servicing B2B and B2C SaaS companies.
Specifically from 2001 to last year, was a, last spring, I was a content marketing manager at a content marketing agency called Animals, which works with a lot of B2B and B2C SaaS companies.
They're well known for...
So high quality content because they have like a really rigorous process or they used to.
think they're now changing it because of AI.
But when I was there, it was just me working directly with an editor and a copy editor and sometimes a researcher.

And so instead of just like writing an article and then sending the draft out, we had a process where it was like you'd write a 10% outline, which is just an outline of the full article, send it to the editor of your junior writer, and then the editor will send it back to you with feedback and comments.
Then you move on to the 30% outline, which is like 450, 500 words of the initial outline.
Once you get the feedback, you send it, you create it, you create a 90% draft, send it back to the editor, and then they send it back to you, send it to the copy editor.
So I did this for about four to five clients, mostly in tech, like Vimeo, Buffer, SaaS companies, ClearScope, which is an SEO tool.
So it was a very rigorous writing and editorial process, and this was before AI.
At the time, I didn't...
I didn't really enjoy the process because it involved like a lot of writing and et cetera and feedback.
But now I really enjoy it and I really appreciate it because now I work a lot more with AI.
And that process at Animals really taught me how to not only write quality content, but how to recognize quality content and how to edit quality content.
When Animals laid off a bunch of its staff in 2023, I stayed on as a freelancer until last year.
And then they started working a lot more with AI.
And at the time I was worried like, oh, no, AI is going to take over my work.
What am I going to do?
Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
That whole fear and doom and gloom.
But then I decided, you know, instead of like beating it, why don't you just join them?
So I decided to learn about how to use AI.
One of my clients at Animals, who I still work with now, is a generative writing platform called Writer.
So what I like about them is that I write for them.
And they insist that we use their...
Their own AI tool to write content for them.
So when companies were saying, no, we don't want to work with the writers who use AI, they really embraced it.
So from there, I learned how to actually use AI in my own processes.
And that encouraged me to learn more about AI.
And then I've continued freelancing, not only for Ryder, who I just mentioned, but I also work for an affiliate marketing agency called Impact.com.
And I also blend my own editorial and human voice and insights using AI, like ChatGPT, Cloud.
So that's really helped.
And then I'll say that's what I do 80% of the time now.
I create content and I collaborate with AI to create content.
And then on the other 20% of the time, I consider myself like an AI agent builder.
I've been learning how to build AI agents and workflows.
For example, a workflow that can post, can create content for you and post content across LinkedIn, Instagram, Twitter, et cetera.
So I've really enjoyed not only learning.
How to work with AI, but actually building my own AI workflows and systems and tools using different tools like Make.com, N18, MindStudio.
don't know if you're, I'm sorry, I'm going up into a tangent like tech and geek talk.
It's yeah.
That's right.

I can interject something along that tangent, which I do want to hear about, which is talk to me about like the practical ways that you're using AI tooling to create content or edit it or put it together.
Any tooling or workflows that you have created for yourself, I'm anxious to hear about those.
Just to characterize it for you, like we view half of the job as content, of course, and people that have a hip pocket understanding of what good content looks and feels like.
But the other half, and in fact, probably 51% or more, is articulating that, which you've explained a little bit about, you know, your thoughts on that, which is great.
And then helping to improve those processes over time.
Because if you're working with content managers, they are managing the pipelines and the pipelines are who we view as the right.
So the pipelines are doing the writing and architecting the content, but then the content managers have to manipulate the inputs, the context, and then see what the desired outputs are.

And then as an ME, part of your job is to help them, of course, do the whole project management and content quality thing, but then also to help them where they come to loggerheads or issues or quirks of an automated pipeline.
One, it just, we're looking for people with some experience in using AI tooling for content work so that they can then observe and help to clear those blockers, right?
And it's not like nobody's coming to help, the engine team is there and everybody's there, I'm there, et cetera.
But on a day-to-day basis, it's like, oh, okay, got it.
Like getting feedback from CMs, how they're working with their writers, and then helping them to say like, hey, maybe we should engineer this context better.
Or maybe, you know, your inputs, aka prompts, or other instructions to this are being misinterpreted.
So let's work on the semantics.
Right.
And that kind of thing.
So love to hear about any experience you have doing that kind of work.
Okay.
So how I approach the writing that I do for my clients is I have what I call like editorial writing guidelines that I feed the AI, whether it's like chat GPT or clause.
So it has like, this is how I prefer articles to be structured.
So for example, impact.com has like branding guidelines and tone of voice.
So I take that and I feed it to the AI.
And then I also ask specific things like, you know, AI has certain words that it likes to use all the time that you can identify in other people's writing on LinkedIn, like certain words.
So I'll put in like, don't use these words, et cetera.
And then I have a prompt like act as a content marketing manager, like growthx.ai.
I've uploaded like these editorial writing guidelines.
I've uploaded like the style guidelines.
I've uploaded the SEO brief.
I've uploaded the, I've uploaded the outline for the article, write the article 2000, 2500 words.
then I send, then I send it out into the chat GPT or Claude.
And then once it's written the article,
go through the article manually, and I actually give it feedback, so using a voice prompt, so you know how directors will be like, okay, cut, do this again, looks great.
So I'll be like, okay, the introduction looks great, but we need to change the tone of voice to be more in line with that, or can you give me five versions that are in line with that?
So it's like, now I'm collaborating with the tool to make sure that, you know, it's giving me the nuance and the little things I want.
Because even from the beginning, even if you still input it, sometimes I'll find that it won't follow the instructions completely.
Maybe, like, maybe we'll get it, like, 80%, but these are things I will need to, like, manually check and make sure that it gives me what I want.
So that'll take maybe, like, 30 minutes, an hour, five minutes, 10 minutes, whatever.

And then when that's finally done, I also have a final checklist, like, just make sure that, you know, H2s are formatted properly, you know, make sure that sentences aren't too long, make sure that words aren't repeated too much.
And then when that's done, I send it back.
And then throughout the process, editors or writers or the managers...
Impact.com will give me feedback.
Oh, we like this.
We like the tone of voice.
We like the style.
Maybe you're using too many of these ones.
So I take that feedback and I keep adding it to that initial editing and writing guidelines doc that I have.
So that each time I go back, I now have like structured feedback they've given me so I can continue feeding it back to the AI.
So now I sort of like have the perfect framework and prompts to give based on what the team has said that they like.
So I like to think of it as a collaboration between myself and the AI to give like the type of quality that Impact.com and my other clients are looking for.
Yeah, great.
Yeah, thank you.
And then have you worked with, like in terms of setting up a workspace for a client, have you worked with any of the following?
Like workflow tools to create processes that perform multiple steps in a row?
Or things like cloud projects to set up a contextual space for you to work.
On client documentation or custom GPTs, if it's like on the chat GPT side or other versions of that kind of thing.
Have you worked with any of those projects?
I've actually created my own AI agent through MindStudio, which is like that checklist that I told you.
So I just upload the article and then it has that checklist.
So it's that kind of like workflow.
So it saves me.
I've taught myself how to build the workflow, but now it also costs in terms of like, it just goes through the article and then checks and then highlights everything that I need to change.
Yeah, yeah.
That makes sense.
And that's exactly what I was asking about because it's, you know, it's essentially what we do, but it doesn't mean that there isn't room to, for people to be doing that ad hoc.
Because like we're extremely tools agnostic, right?
We don't care really what it takes to get the job done.
And something like a MindStudio, a Zapier, or a Make.com is basically a prototyper for the kinds of workflows that we do build.
And so people that have had some experience thinking that way will have a better time.
Kind of understanding the way that Atlas works and the way that we function.
So it can be very helpful knowledge to have, even if you've done it casually.
And it seems like you've done it in the scope of your work, which is even better.
Because working on it with content in mind does matter, you know.
Have you, you mentioned having worked with teams of writers.
Have you managed writers before or teams to deliver client content?
Or have you always worked as an IC?
Do you have any experience there?
No, I do have like, way back in my career, like I used to work at a startup agency.
I graduated like in 2008, 2011.
And then I worked at a startup agency for one year.
And then they promoted me to like manage other teams, which I did for a year because they were growing so quickly.
But that was 10 years ago.
And since then, I haven't had any other experience.
only been working with my, working with the team.
Like, you know, having someone edit my work or working directly with the client where they give me feedback.
And we collaborate together.
But in terms of like managing.
Content managers, no, I haven't done that specifically in my career.
Okay.
And then on the client deliverable side, obviously, you know, all client deliverables and any client work always comes with attendant deadlines and that sort of thing.
Can you talk to me a little bit about your, like, process that you, by which you organize yourself and your work to meet deadlines and to keep things on schedule and that sort of thing?
And just talk about your, the architect of your, your project management system.
Just mostly, basically just tools, mostly Asana and Slack.
Like, I love using Asana because you can break down the project according to, like, timelines.
So, for example, today is September 10th, if something is due at the end of the month, I know that, okay, let's get the outline sent to the client by the end of this week.
Let's make sure that the client gives us feedback by the end of next week.
So, breaking it down into projects and also having the client on board as well so that they can sign off on things because sometimes I am noticed.
It's not always our fault.
Sometimes it's the client that is the bottleneck.
They're not giving feedback.
So having them involved and then following up with them, whether it's through Asana and Slack, like, hey, I haven't heard back from you on the outline and the deadlines by the end of the month.
So just having that scheduling tool like Asana where you're both kind of like that transparency for myself and for them.
And then also just being proactive with tools like Asana and Slack just for communicating and even just letting them know that, you know, we're behind by a couple of days.
it okay if we're behind to next week or something like that?
So I really like using those kind of internal tools like Asana and Slack.
Yeah, that's great.
Yeah, we use a combination of tools, but we do use linear quite a bit.
That's mostly internal facing, though.
On the client side, we use a combination of things like Airtable and Notion and, of course, shared Slack channels.
So, you know, nothing incredibly esoteric or weird, but if you've used Asana, you're going to be fine.
Yeah, and Notion I've used, Airtable I've also used.
We use that a lot at Animals as well.
Yeah.
I've built with Airtable, so it's easy.
Not easy, but I know how to use it.
Yeah, exactly.
Airtable is very complex.
But it's fun.
Like, the basics are easy, but then it's very deep.
You know, it is weird because it's incredibly programmable, but with almost no interfaces that make any sense.
Like, they need a whole, like, overhaul of all of their UX, but they're never going to do it.
But anyway, long story short, it's very, very customizable.
And, you you can build automations for it for Slack.

Like, for instance, when we have some clients where we've published articles and we move them to the published status, it will create a digest of those and send it to the Slack channel so that we're not responsible for, like, remembering to say, hey, we published these five or whatever.
And they're not responsible for checking the Airtable, yet another Airtable, which I'm sure they're already checking multiple internally.
So, yeah, that's good.
All of that tracks well.
Yeah, yeah,
Yeah.

was, let me, what was it, and then, oh, yeah.
So you mentioned you have worked in client contexts.

Have you ever worked in client relationships or management directly, like working with external clients while you were at an agency like animals or in your personal work, you've worked directly with stakeholders on the client side?
Yeah.
As a freelancer, so both before animals and then after animals.
my work with impact.com and Ryder, they're my clients.
So, yeah, I work directly with them.
They give me the feedback.
We work on deadlines and everything, setting quality standards, et cetera.
So I actually prefer that as opposed to actually having, like, a project manager or a client manager because I understand the process and the writing and the content.
So it's nice to be able to go directly to the client and if there are issues or questions that are needed to ask for clarification, yeah, that's fine.
And presenting reports and presenting.
Presenting pitches and presenting outlines, I have experience doing that as well.
Okay, good.
Yeah, report, obviously, client management is part of the ME job, especially in the steady state.

You know, we have clients that go through a sprint process where they may work with a sprint team to kind of build out the initial kind of scopes of work and work streams that we're doing for them, deciding exactly what to build and building and architecting the tooling.
But then once they come out of sprint, there is a director that is assigned to that account manager style director that is assigned to that client.
But the weekly meetings and the day-to-day and the communication back and forth, that falls to the ME.
So it's good.
We want people with that, have had that experience before.

Because as you know, it just requires a different mode of thinking or a mode of speaking and communicating to make sure that things are clear to the client, but then also to keep them engaged and to remind them every day that we're doing these things that they don't want to do and we're doing a good job and that we're reporting clearly and that we can contextualize.

and say, hey, you know, we're seeing some signs of life in these content verticals or clusters, and we would like to pursue these more, and so we're making, therefore, we're making these recommendations about the kind of things that we want to do next, right?

And that idea of it's not it's not pure sales, but you're always soft selling when in client relations, you're always trying to make sure that they view you as a valued partner, that you are taking work off of their plate, not putting work on their plate, and that you are thinking about things for them to do in the future with you.
And I think that's like a key thing, because, you know, we have I think our contracts are like 12 months and most of our engagements are on that scale.
But at the same time, you do have to remind people why they are using, you know, and sometimes that's a reminder of like, hey, here's all the advanced tooling that we built.
me walk you through this, what we've built for you as a client, which you subtext being you don't want to you don't want to do this.
Right.
You don't want.
And then the other parts are, we are thinking strategically and tactically about the work that we are doing for you because we know what your goals are.
And this is how they will drive to your goals.
Or to push back and say, you know what, I understand where you're coming from, but are you sure that this is going to align with these goals?
And if not, let's find another way to slice that apple for you and get you to a place where you are actually driving towards the goals you've expressed to us.
And then there are the steady state updates and things that happen in the normal flow of work.
Like they are telling you on a call something about their, like a product that they're launching, right?
Or a new product line or some new initiative or something like that.
And the active listening part of ME's job is, oh, interesting.
Tell me more about that.
If you have information to share about that, we can start preparing ourselves to be able to write about, A, just write about that product, you know, for you to build out content support.
But then also to update our contextual artifacts to support these new approaches that you're doing, whether it's new product or changing company positioning or anything like that.
Our artifacts need to be updated internally, like your artifact that you mentioned, your writing guideline that you've created for that client.
It would need to be updated if they have changed their tone or positioning or ICP of the person that they're selling to or their product is changing significantly, obviously.
We've startups, startups pivot constantly.
So if you're about to pivot, let us know.
We will pivot your content for you or pivot your strategy for you so that you're prepared for those new worlds.
And if you have never done that work before, it becomes difficult to suddenly learn that on the fly.

So it's always helpful if you've done some of that to work with clients before because it's part of the job to make sure that we are an active participant so that they don't view us as an accessory easily discarded.
And instead, we're an active participant in their strategy.
Yeah, I 100%.
I agree with that.
And that's the approach that I try and take with my work as well.
I mentioned that to Andy as well.
I spoke to her last night that I don't think of myself as, I think of myself as a partner, not just someone who's like a prompt monkey or whatever, just who does, you know, things.
And that's the relationship that I had, for example, with Carrie, who now works at growthx.ai.
She was my editor for two years.

And we used to have this whole back and forth, like, you know what, like, I'll push back if I didn't think that something was still wasn't in alignment for what the client was looking for, or what the client wanted, even though, you know, the brief said something.
So sometimes something would get sent back to the client for extra feedback or clarification, they'll be sent back to me and we'd work on it.
So I also think of it in terms of that.
It's not just, you know, I will do what you want, so I'll take the money and then that's it.
It's like, the end goal is like the quality, right?
Producing quality work that meets the client's goals, but also meets the audience's needs as well.
Yeah.

Yeah, because, you know, it all pays forward to not only just helping them achieve their goals, which makes us feel better and look better in their eyes, but then also just when it comes time for contract renewal, they're like, hey, this person's been an active participant in our growth.
You know, they've been really trying to help us to move forward.
And therefore, we want to keep working with them.
want to keep them in our circle, you know, so that's a good thing.
Do you have any questions for me?
Anything I can answer for you?
Yeah, I would just love to learn more about, because now we're seeing a lot more content shops who combine in human editorial, you know, insights and partnerships with like AI.
What makes GrowthX different from like the other emerging agencies that are coming up that are doing the same thing?
Yeah.
I think one big anchoring principle is that we build our own tools.
We don't rely on external tooling or a patchwork of it to provide the core services that we offer, although we are agnostic when it comes to using those tools to either.
need the pre

Prototype functions or to get work done in the interim, but it's all in service of improving our product because we do feel that in the long term, we are really well positioned to use the client work that we're doing as ground truth for what the actual work being done is.

And the actual processes that need to be articulated, pulled apart, and then productized, rather than building something in a vacuum for people that are like, oh, this sort of fits what I need, but now the burden is on me to manipulate it into a shape that actually functions for this business process.
And we're starting with content because it is a great place to begin.
It's a very difficult business process or a very difficult creative process that is incredibly undocumented.
Like, I know Animals has done a lot of work to create an internal content framework.
I have seen it.
It's all right.
Like, it misses a lot, in my opinion.
And that's obviously just my opinion, but...
The world is made up of opinions.
We're going to have them until we come up with a product that we think works for people.
Right now, it's working for us.
It's delivering work for clients, and it's functioning well.

We started with content because it is a thorny problem, and there are obviously many more threads for us to pull before we get to a place of extreme confidence where we can say like, hey, the public can now use this platform.
Let's go scale to thousands or millions of customers that can utilize the platform to create content for themselves.
But for now, it is delivering work internally, providing real value.
We're driving real business.
We're profitable.
This is not some sort of ghost operation that's floating on VC money and just burning cash in the hopes of delivering something someday.
And the pay forward to this is that if we are able to really articulate in a fine-tuned way the process by which content gets ideated,
Scoped, drafted, and refined, we feel that the architecture that we built with Atlas, which is our internal tool, can be applied to other business functions.

And so if you can imagine a sales function, how much happens between an initial outreach and a signed contract or even deeper into like a steady state client, like that whole process, how much of that lives in a salesperson's head?
Or in a collection of Google documents that's unorganized or in like scraps in Notion or Gong or HubSpot or all over the place.

And that process by which all of that information was ingested, by which the client was wooed and won and established and delivered for is nebulous and lives inside people's heads and lives in the ether or in a collection of unconnected documents.
And we view Atlas as...

A way to articulate those processes, to define that messy middle, and to help make it a repeatable, realized process that not anybody, people have to be smart and everybody brings something new to the table.

That's why humans are in the loop, but a vast majority more people can take advantage of, because if you have like an amazing salesperson who just kills it and is delivering deals and really like just, wow, they're 10x what the next person is down.

That's great, but when that process, that process happens usually from between here and here, like with a good writer in content, you hand them a prospectus and they come back with something, you're, wow, these are amazing narrative flourishes.
This is an incredible job you've done articulating this in a logical framework.
How did you do it?
And they're like, I don't know, you know, I did, I did some stuff in here and then it came out the other end like this.
And like that, that process is, it's great.
And I'm glad we're glad.
We have those people while we have them.
But when they leave, that whole body of data and process and all of that stuff also vanishes.
So it's just like, hey, what if we could apply this kind of articulation to other business processes?
And we feel that a variety of them would benefit from this process, whether that be like sales or marketing or HR or internal systems management.
then like just there's tons of opportunities for this to kind of expand beyond just, hey, this is a content engine.
And I think right now there's a lot of people in the content world building content engines.
And they are going to fall short because they're not thinking of it in a systemic enough manner.
They're thinking of it in terms of like pure inputs and outputs, and they're not building enough articulation points along the path.
And so you end up trying to like turn on a light switch with a broomstick instead of turning on a light switch with a well-articulated finger that has eyes attached.
So it understands the context in which it operates and it can go flick it on, you know, again, that's, that's.
Kind of where we're headed.
And yeah, we're not totally there.
That's why there's a lot of humans still here, hand cranking a lot of things.
But I can tell you when it works, it's insane.

It's like really magical to watch an idea go into a pipeline, the research be done, the drafting and articulation be done, and then something coming out the other end that is a handful of editorial minutes away from delivering to a client with confidence.
And it's cool.
It works.
And when it works, it's beautiful.
When it doesn't work, it's like, well, I guess I'll go bare knuckle this, you know what mean, until it's deliverable.
How are you managing that, you know, you have those goals and the systems and the processes and you're working towards that?
And you guys are growing because I noticed you've got your funding and everything.
But you're also producing content for quality for clients.
So I imagine there must be like some, must be a lot of growing pains and moving parts and all of that.
Like, how are you managing all of that?
And how are the creators and the team?
team managing and taking that on as well.
Yeah, exactly.
High communication, clear transparency from the team.
I don't think you'll find a more transparent leadership team.
You know, Daniel and Marcel are very open about sharing exactly what is going on.
Our EPD channels, our developers channels, the engineering channels, all of that stuff is like there in Slack.
I read it every day.
It's like a book of cool things coming, you know, like they're working on all this stuff.
It's not like it doesn't happen in a black box that's isolated from the delivery org.
You know, the delivery org has access to those engineers to like file a quick ticket and say like, oh, my pipeline's acting funky.
And like there's an engineer on call that goes in and says, oh, you know, I think it's this.
Let me fix that for you.
And that, of course, adds to the value of the product, right?
So on the pluses side, there's good transparency, clear communication.
Everybody understands that we are building a thing, building on the fly.
And therefore, expectations have to be managed.
On the negative side, it's messy at times because the demand is elastic and the capabilities of the platform are elastic.
You know, a client may come in like, and we're like, cool, we just booked a huge, you know, scope of work.
We just booked a client for, you know, $200,000 a month.
Oh, that's amazing.
Yay.
We're so happy we won that deal.
And then you start thinking, what are we going to to deliver for $2,000 a month, right?
And then you go like, okay, well, that's the elasticity.
Maybe part of that is hand-cranked and human-driven until we build the tooling, right?
And of course, the sprint process, which is like the first 12 weeks in an engagement, it's all about trying to build as much of the tooling as we can.
There's always some elasticity in what a client wants and what we're able to deliver via tooling and then also via humans.
So that is the negative aspect.
Don't come here if you aren't prepared to deal with that elasticity.
It's never like antagonistic and there's no animosity.
Nobody's like writing anybody about it.
We all understand it.
But it is capacity, right?
And some people like steady state work where they're like, I know I have a finite amount of deliverables.
I have a really established process to get those deliverables out and I will deliver those every week.
And that is cool.
And they're just executors.
Cool.
Nothing wrong with people like that.
But in a startup, the elasticity means that you will at times be overextended.
And then at times you'll feel like the king of the world.
You know, you'd be like, I could I could do all of this work in an afternoon.
This is amazing.
